Definition: A fully managed serverless ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service that discovers, catalogs, cleans, transforms, and moves data between data stores — supporting Python/Scala Spark jobs, streaming ETL, and visual job authoring.

A centralized metadata repository that stores table definitions, schema information, and partition metadata for data stored in S3, RDS, Redshift, and other sources — serving as the Hive-compatible metastore for Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and EMR.
Automated programs that scan data stores (S3, JDBC databases) to infer schema, detect partitions, and populate the Glue Data Catalog with table definitions — running on-demand or on a schedule.

Serverless Spark-based jobs that extract data from sources, apply transformations (mapping, filtering, joining, aggregating), and load results to targets — authored visually (Studio), via script, or with Glue DataBrew.
A feature that tracks previously processed data so Glue ETL jobs only process new or changed data on subsequent runs — preventing duplicate processing and enabling incremental ETL patterns.

A visual data preparation tool that enables data analysts and scientists to clean, normalize, and transform data using 250+ pre-built transformations — without writing code, with built-in data quality profiling.
A serverless feature that validates and controls the evolution of streaming data schemas — supporting Avro, JSON Schema, and Protobuf formats, integrated with Kinesis Data Streams, MSK, and Glue ETL.

Glue jobs that continuously consume and process streaming data from Kinesis Data Streams or Apache Kafka (MSK) — applying transformations in micro-batches and writing results to S3, Redshift, or other targets.

A real-time data streaming service that captures and processes hundreds of thousands of records per second — data is stored in shards for 24 hours (up to 365 days), consumed by multiple applications simultaneously.
The base throughput unit of a Kinesis stream — each shard provides 1 MB/sec input and 2 MB/sec output. Capacity is scaled by adding/removing shards (resharding), and on-demand mode auto-scales.

A value used to determine which shard a data record is routed to — records with the same partition key go to the same shard, enabling ordered processing per key. Poor key distribution causes hot shards.

A fully managed service that loads streaming data into destinations (S3, Redshift, OpenSearch, Splunk, HTTP endpoints) — with automatic batching, compression, encryption, and optional Lambda transformation, requiring zero administration.

Firehose buffers incoming data before delivering to destinations — configurable by size (1-128 MB) and time (60-900 seconds), whichever condition is met first triggers delivery. Smaller buffers = lower latency, larger = fewer files.

Term: Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka (MSK)
Definition: A fully managed Apache Kafka service for building real-time streaming data pipelines — managing broker provisioning, patching, and cluster operations while you focus on producing and consuming data.
A serverless mode of Amazon MSK that automatically provisions and scales Kafka cluster capacity — eliminating the need to manage broker count, storage, or partitions, with pay-per-use pricing.

A feature that enables you to deploy fully managed Kafka Connect connectors to stream data to and from Apache Kafka clusters — supporting hundreds of pre-built connectors for databases, S3, OpenSearch, and more.

A managed service that migrates databases to AWS with minimal downtime — supporting homogeneous (Oracle→Oracle) and heterogeneous (Oracle→PostgreSQL) migrations, with continuous data replication (CDC) for ongoing sync.

A DMS feature that continuously replicates ongoing changes (inserts, updates, deletes) from source to target databases after the initial full load — enabling near-real-time data synchronization with minimal source impact.

A tool that converts source database schemas, stored procedures, and application code to a format compatible with the target database — used with DMS for heterogeneous migrations (e.g., Oracle to PostgreSQL).

A fully managed integration service that transfers data between SaaS applications (Salesforce, SAP, Slack, ServiceNow) and AWS services (S3, Redshift) — with built-in data transformations, filtering, and validation.

A fully managed service for SFTP, FTPS, and FTP file transfers directly into and out of S3 or EFS — enabling legacy file-transfer workflows to use cloud storage without modifying existing client applications.

A data transfer service that automates moving data between on-premises storage and AWS (S3, EFS, FSx) — supporting scheduled transfers, bandwidth throttling, data integrity verification, and encryption in transit.

A serverless event bus that receives events from AWS services, SaaS applications, and custom sources — routing events to targets (Lambda, Step Functions, Kinesis) based on rules and patterns for event-driven architectures.

An object storage service offering virtually unlimited scalability, 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability, and multiple storage classes — the foundation for data lakes, serving as the central repository for structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data.

Tiered storage options optimized for different access patterns — Standard (frequent access), Intelligent-Tiering (auto-tiering), Standard-IA (infrequent), One Zone-IA (single AZ), Glacier Instant/Flexible/Deep Archive (archival) — each with different cost and retrieval characteristics.

Rules that automatically transition objects between storage classes or expire (delete) them based on age — enabling cost optimization by moving aging data from Standard to IA to Glacier to deletion over time.

A feature that keeps multiple variants of an object in the same bucket — protecting against accidental deletions and overwrites, enabling rollback to previous versions, and required for cross-region replication.

A feature that enforces write-once-read-many (WORM) protection — Governance mode (privileged users can override) and Compliance mode (no one can delete, including root) with configurable retention periods for regulatory compliance.

Server-side encryption options — SSE-S3 (Amazon-managed keys), SSE-KMS (AWS KMS-managed keys with audit trail), SSE-C (customer-provided keys), and client-side encryption (encrypt before upload). Default encryption can be set at bucket level.

Features that use SQL expressions to retrieve only a subset of data from an object — filtering rows and columns at the storage layer before returning results, reducing data transfer and improving query performance.
Automated triggers when objects are created, deleted, or modified in S3 — sending events to Lambda, SQS, SNS, or EventBridge to initiate downstream processing pipelines.

A centralized repository (typically S3) that stores structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data at any scale in its raw format — enabling diverse analytics (SQL queries, ML, real-time) without upfront schema definition.

A service that simplifies building, securing, and managing data lakes — providing centralized permissions (fine-grained access control at column/row/cell level), data cataloging, ETL job creation, and cross-account data sharing.

A centralized permission model that replaces complex IAM and S3 bucket policies — granting table, column, row, and cell-level access to databases and tables in the Data Catalog using a simple grant/revoke interface.

A fully managed petabyte-scale cloud data warehouse using columnar storage and massively parallel processing (MPP) — optimized for complex analytical queries across large datasets with automatic tuning and concurrency scaling.

A Redshift feature that queries data directly in S3 without loading it into Redshift tables — using external tables defined in the Glue Data Catalog, enabling queries that join Redshift data with S3 data lake data.

A serverless option for Amazon Redshift that automatically provisions and scales data warehouse capacity — eliminating cluster management while providing the same SQL analytics capabilities with pay-per-use pricing.

Strategies for distributing table data across nodes — KEY (rows with same key on same node for join optimization), EVEN (round-robin), ALL (full copy on every node for small dimension tables), and AUTO (Redshift chooses).

Columns that determine the physical order of data on disk — compound sort keys (ordered by column sequence) and interleaved sort keys (equal weight to each column). Proper sort keys dramatically improve query performance by enabling zone map filtering.

A feature that automatically adds transient cluster capacity when queries are queued — providing consistent query performance during peak demand by routing queries to scaling clusters, with one hour of free credits per day.

Pre-computed result sets stored in Redshift that automatically refresh when base tables change — accelerating repeated complex queries (dashboards, reports) by returning cached results instead of re-executing the full query.

A fully managed serverless NoSQL key-value and document database — providing single-digit millisecond performance at any scale, with automatic scaling, built-in security, backup/restore, and global tables for multi-region replication.

Term: DynamoDB - Partition Key and Sort Key
Definition: The primary key structure — partition key (hash key) determines data distribution across partitions, and optional sort key (range key) enables range queries and ordering within a partition. Together they uniquely identify each item.

An ordered flow of item-level change records (insert, update, delete) from a DynamoDB table — enabling event-driven architectures, cross-region replication, and real-time analytics by triggering Lambda functions on data changes.

An index with a different partition key and optional sort key from the base table — enabling queries on non-primary-key attributes with its own provisioned throughput, eventually consistent reads, and projected attributes.

A managed service for relational databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server) — handling provisioning, patching, backups, and failover while you manage the schema, queries, and application logic.

A MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible relational database with up to 5x MySQL and 3x PostgreSQL performance — featuring auto-scaling storage (up to 128 TB), 15 read replicas, multi-AZ by default, and Aurora Serverless for variable workloads.

The process of coordinating and scheduling the execution of data processing tasks in the correct order — managing dependencies, retries, error handling, and monitoring across complex multi-step data workflows.

A serverless orchestration service that coordinates multiple AWS services into workflows using visual state machines — supporting sequential, parallel, branching, and error-handling patterns for data pipeline orchestration.

Term: Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow (MWAA)
Definition: A managed service for Apache Airflow that orchestrates complex data pipelines using Python DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs) — scheduling, monitoring, and managing dependencies across ETL jobs, queries, and ML workflows.

A Glue feature that orchestrates multiple crawlers, jobs, and triggers into a single workflow — defining execution dependencies, parallel execution, and conditional logic for end-to-end ETL pipeline automation.

Components that start Glue ETL jobs and crawlers — supporting scheduled triggers (cron), on-demand triggers (manual/API), and conditional triggers (start when another job succeeds/fails) for pipeline automation.

A monitoring and observability service that collects metrics, logs, and events from AWS resources — providing dashboards, alarms, anomaly detection, and automated actions for data pipeline monitoring and alerting.

Automated alerts triggered when metrics cross defined thresholds — supporting static thresholds, anomaly detection bands, composite alarms (combining multiple alarms), and actions (SNS notifications, Auto Scaling, Lambda).
A governance and audit service that records all API calls made in your AWS account — providing a complete history of who did what, when, and from where for compliance auditing and security analysis.

A fully managed pub/sub messaging service for sending notifications — supporting email, SMS, HTTP/HTTPS, SQS, and Lambda subscribers for alerting on data pipeline failures, completions, and anomalies.

A fully managed message queuing service that decouples and buffers data between pipeline components — supporting standard queues (best-effort ordering, at-least-once delivery) and FIFO queues (exactly-once, ordered processing).

The practice of ensuring data is accurate, complete, consistent, timely, and valid — measured through profiling, validation rules, and monitoring, with AWS Glue Data Quality providing automated rule-based quality checks.

A Glue feature that automatically recommends and evaluates data quality rules — checking completeness, uniqueness, accuracy, and custom conditions, with results published to CloudWatch and integrated into ETL workflows.

The ability to track data from its origin through all transformations to its final destination — understanding where data came from, how it was transformed, and where it is consumed for debugging, compliance, and impact analysis.

The practice of managing and provisioning data infrastructure through code rather than manual processes — using AWS CloudFormation or AWS CDK to version-control, replicate, and automate data pipeline deployments.

An IaC service that provisions AWS resources using declarative JSON/YAML templates — enabling repeatable, version-controlled deployment of entire data pipeline stacks (Glue jobs, Redshift clusters, S3 buckets, IAM roles).

Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment practices applied to data engineering — automating testing, validation, and deployment of ETL code, schema changes, and pipeline configurations using CodePipeline and CodeBuild.

The property that running a pipeline multiple times with the same input produces the same result — critical for retry logic and failure recovery, achieved through techniques like UPSERT operations, deduplication, and checkpoint tracking.

The process of retroactively processing historical data through a pipeline — required when adding new transformations, fixing bugs, or onboarding new data consumers who need access to historical processed data.

The service that controls authentication and authorization for AWS resources — using users, groups, roles, and policies to define who can access which data resources and what actions they can perform.
IAM roles assumed by AWS services (Glue, Redshift, EMR) to access data — following least-privilege principles by granting only the specific S3, KMS, and Glue Catalog permissions each service needs.
A managed service for creating and controlling encryption keys — used to encrypt data at rest in S3, Redshift, DynamoDB, RDS, and Glue, with key policies controlling who can use and manage each key.

Protecting stored data by encrypting it on disk — S3 (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C), Redshift (KMS or HSM), DynamoDB (AWS-owned or customer-managed KMS keys), RDS (KMS), and Glue (KMS for Data Catalog and job outputs).
Protecting data as it moves between services — using TLS/SSL for all AWS API calls, HTTPS for S3 access, SSL for database connections, and VPC endpoints to keep traffic on the AWS private network.

Resource-based JSON policies attached to S3 buckets that control access — specifying which principals (users, accounts, services) can perform which actions on which objects, supporting cross-account access and conditional restrictions.

Named network endpoints with dedicated access policies attached to S3 buckets — simplifying data access management for shared datasets by creating application-specific entry points with tailored permissions.
Interface and gateway endpoints that enable private connectivity to AWS data services (S3, DynamoDB, Glue, Redshift, Kinesis) without internet access — keeping data traffic within the AWS network.

A data security service that uses ML to automatically discover, classify, and protect sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials) stored in S3 — providing alerts and detailed findings for data privacy compliance.

A service that securely stores, rotates, and retrieves database credentials, API keys, and other secrets — enabling applications and ETL jobs to access credentials programmatically without hardcoding them.

The ability to grant or restrict access to specific columns in Redshift tables — preventing users from querying sensitive columns (SSN, salary) while allowing access to non-sensitive columns in the same table.

The ability to restrict which rows a user can see based on their identity — using RLS policies that filter query results so users only see data they are authorized to access (e.g., their own region's data).

Centralized permissions in Lake Formation that control access at the database, table, column, row, and cell level — replacing complex combinations of IAM policies, S3 bucket policies, and Glue Catalog resource policies.

The technique of obscuring sensitive data values while preserving data format and usability — using methods like tokenization, hashing, redaction, or substitution to protect PII in non-production environments and analytics.

CloudTrail logs all data access and management API calls — tracking who accessed which S3 objects, who ran which Glue jobs, who queried which Redshift tables, providing the audit trail required for data governance.

The process of categorizing data by sensitivity level (public, internal, confidential, restricted) — determining appropriate security controls, encryption requirements, access policies, and retention rules for each category.

Patterns for securely sharing data between AWS accounts — using S3 bucket policies, Lake Formation cross-account grants, Redshift data sharing, and AWS RAM to enable controlled access without data duplication.

A Redshift feature that enables live, read-only access to Redshift data across clusters and accounts without copying — producer clusters share databases/schemas/tables, and consumer clusters query them with up-to-date results.

A serverless interactive query service that analyzes data directly in S3 using standard SQL — with no infrastructure to manage, pay-per-query pricing, and integration with the Glue Data Catalog for schema management.

Organizing S3 data into partition folders (e.g., year=2024/month=01/) that Athena uses to skip irrelevant data — dramatically reducing the amount of data scanned and lowering query costs and execution time.

Columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) dramatically outperform row-based formats (CSV, JSON) in Athena — enabling column pruning (read only needed columns) and better compression, reducing data scanned by 30-90%.
A statement that creates a new table from query results — enabling data format conversion (CSV to Parquet), partitioning, compression, and bucketing in a single operation for query optimization.

A feature that separates users, teams, or applications into groups with individual query settings — enabling per-workgroup cost controls (data scan limits), query result locations, and CloudWatch metrics.
A managed big data platform for running Apache Spark, Hive, Presto, HBase, and other frameworks — processing vast amounts of data across resizable EC2 clusters or serverless with EMR Serverless.

Three node types — Primary (manages cluster, coordinates tasks), Core (runs tasks and stores HDFS data, cannot be removed), and Task (runs tasks only, no HDFS storage, can be added/removed for scaling with Spot Instances).

A serverless option for EMR that automatically provisions and scales compute resources for Spark and Hive jobs — eliminating cluster management while providing the same open-source framework capabilities.

A distributed processing engine for large-scale data analytics — supporting batch processing, streaming (Structured Streaming), SQL (Spark SQL), ML (MLlib), and graph processing (GraphX) on EMR clusters.
A data warehouse framework that provides SQL-like queries (HiveQL) over large datasets in S3 or HDFS — using the Glue Data Catalog as its metastore for schema management and partition tracking.

A serverless, ML-powered business intelligence service for creating interactive dashboards and visualizations — supporting SPICE in-memory engine, embedded analytics, natural language queries (Q), and pay-per-session pricing.

Super-fast, Parallel, In-memory Calculation Engine — QuickSight's in-memory storage that imports data for fast dashboard performance, with automatic replication across AZs and capacity measured in GB per user.

A dimensional modeling pattern with a central fact table (transactions, events) surrounded by dimension tables (products, customers, dates) — optimized for analytical queries with simple joins and fast aggregations.

An extension of star schema where dimension tables are normalized into sub-dimensions — reducing data redundancy at the cost of more complex joins, used when dimension data is very large or frequently updated.

Techniques for handling changes to dimension data over time — Type 1 (overwrite), Type 2 (add new row with version/date tracking), Type 3 (add column for previous value), with Type 2 being most common for historical analysis.

An open-source columnar storage format optimized for analytics — storing data by column rather than row, enabling efficient compression and column pruning, and the preferred format for Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and Spark.

Optimized Row Columnar format — a columnar storage format popular with Hive and Presto, providing efficient compression, predicate pushdown, and built-in indexes for fast analytical queries on large datasets.
A row-based data serialization format with embedded schema — ideal for write-heavy workloads, streaming data, and schema evolution scenarios where the schema changes over time (fields added/removed).
Techniques for organizing data into logical segments — by date (year/month/day), by category, or by hash for even distribution — reducing query scan scope in Athena, Redshift Spectrum, and Spark.

The process of combining many small files into fewer larger files — improving query performance in S3-based analytics (Athena, Spark) by reducing the overhead of opening/reading thousands of tiny files.
